texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Although research and development studies to improve commercialized reverse osmosis and thermally-driven desalination processes are continuing, there exists a need to develop and evaluate alternate desalination technologies, e.g., membrane distillation (MD), which can be driven by low-grade waste heat if available. In one variety of MD (Fig. 1a), direct contact membrane distillation (DCMD), hot brine flows on one side of a gas-filled porous hydrophobic hollow fiber membrane and cold distillate flows on the other side of the membrane; water vapor evaporated from hot brine is recovered in the cold distillate stream by condensation. This technique is of interest here.

texts.text: a b s t r a c t

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A small pilot plant for direct contact membrane distillation (DCMD) based desalination was built and operated successfully on a daily basis for 3 months. The operation employed hot brine at 64-93 ◦ C and distillate at 20-54 ◦ C. The hot brine was either city water, city water containing salt at the level of 3.5, 6 or 10%, or sea water trucked in from Long Island Sound, CT. One to ten horizontal crossflow hollow fiber membrane modules each having either 2448 or 2652 hollow fibers and 0.61 or 0.66 m 2 surface area were combined in various configurations to study the plant performance. The highest water vapor flux of 55kg/(m 2 h) was achieved with two modules in series; the flux varied between 15 and 33 kg/(m 2 h) for configurations employing 6-10 modules. The highest distillate production rate achieved was 0.62 gallons per minute (gpm). The membrane modules never showed any sign of distillate contamination by salt. The plant operated successfully with a very limited flux reduction at salt concentrations up to 19.5% from sea water. A mathematical model was successfully developed to describe the performance of the pilot plant with multiple crossflow modules in different test configurations.
texts.text: A small pilot plant for direct contact membrane distillation (DCMD) based desalination was built and operated successfully on a daily basis for 3 months. The operation employed hot brine at 64-93 ◦ C and distillate at 20-54 ◦ C. The hot brine was either city water, city water containing salt at the level of 3.5, 6 or 10%, or sea water trucked in from Long Island Sound, CT. One to ten horizontal crossflow hollow fiber membrane modules each having either 2448 or 2652 hollow fibers and 0.61 or 0.66 m 2 surface area were combined in various configurations to study the plant performance. The highest water vapor flux of 55kg/(m 2 h) was achieved with two modules in series; the flux varied between 15 and 33 kg/(m 2 h) for configurations employing 6-10 modules. The highest distillate production rate achieved was 0.62 gallons per minute (gpm). The membrane modules never showed any sign of distillate contamination by salt. The plant operated successfully with a very limited flux reduction at salt concentrations up to 19.5% from sea water. A mathematical model was successfully developed to describe the performance of the pilot plant with multiple crossflow modules in different test configurations.

texts.orig: A number of questions arose as we evaluated the data from the pilot plant. We have recently developed a detailed model to predict the DCMD performance of an individual crossflow module at two scales, 119 and 2864 cm 2 of membrane surface area [2]. First, it is of interest to know whether this model can predict the performance of a single larger module of this study as well as the performances of a collection/assembly of larger modules in various configurations.

texts.orig: There were additional items of considerable interest: (1) the quality of distilled water being produced in this much larger configuration; (2) the possibility of the system developing leakage over the extended period of run; (3) the system behavior with sea water in the continuous seawater desalination run with a fixed volume of sea water; (4) the nature of the flux behavior as the sea water was being concentrated two to five times. The DCMD system behavior vis-à-vis these items will influence strongly the possibility of DCMD carving out a niche in the desalination marketplace.

It was reasonably successful in predicting the experimentally observed performances for small laboratory scale [1] and larger bench-scale crossflow DCMD modules [2]. This model was also used for a process analysis to design a countercurrent cascade of crossflow modules [3]. The following assumptions were used to develop the model:
texts.text: In our previous paper [2] a mathematical model of crossflow DCMD modules was developed. It was reasonably successful in predicting the experimentally observed performances for small laboratory scale [1] and larger bench-scale crossflow DCMD modules [2]. texts.orig: For the next layer of fibers (( j + 1)th layer), a basic assumption is made, namely, there is no lateral mixing of the hot brine in the x -direction between two successive fiber layers. The hot brine velocity and temperature imposed on the ( j + 1)th layer of fibers therefore depend on x , the fiber length coordinate and are obtained as output from the calculations for the previous layer ( j th layer) of fibers. The calculation process is thus continued till the last fiber layer in a given module. The computational scheme adopted proceeds from one fiber layer to the next layer for the hot brine flow rate coming in at the first fiber layer; further, there is a constant distillate flow rate and temperature input into all fibers in all layers. There can be small variations in distillate side flow rate with the fiber layer number due to the following reason: with the increasing layer number, the brine temperature decreases leading to a lower temperature rise in the distillate, therefore an increasing viscosity and consequently a lower flow rate since the pressures at the fiber inlet and outlet on the distillate side are essentially same regardless of the layer number. For each fiber layer the quantities V p0, j (= V d0 / m , where m is the number of fiber layers) and T p0, j (= T d0 ), are constant for given distillate feed conditions (i.e., inlet volumetric flow rate V d0 and temperature T d0 ). The distillate outlet volumetric flow rate V d1 is the sum of V p1, j for all fiber layers, and the distillate outlet temperature T d1 is the flow weighted average of T p1, j for all fiber layers based on energy balance.

The computational scheme adopted proceeds from one fiber layer to the next layer for the hot brine flow rate coming in at the first fiber layer; further, there is a constant distillate flow rate and temperature input into all fibers in all layers. There can be small variations in distillate side flow rate with the fiber layer number due to the following reason: with the increasing layer number, the brine temperature decreases leading to a lower temperature rise in the distillate, therefore an increasing viscosity and consequently a lower flow rate since the pressures at the fiber inlet and outlet on the distillate side are essentially same regardless of the layer number. For each fiber layer the quantities V p0, j (= V d0 / m , where m is the number of fiber layers) and T p0, j (= T d0 ), are constant for given distillate feed conditions (i.e., inlet volumetric flow rate V d0 and temperature T d0 ). The distillate outlet volumetric flow rate V d1 is the sum of V p1, j for all fiber layers, and the distillate outlet temperature T d1 is the flow weighted average of T p1, j for all fiber layers based on energy balance.

texts.orig: In the pilot scale studies, two rectangular modules were almost always stacked one on top of the other. We have identified such an arrangement as a single-pair unit; this was the most common testing configuration in the pilot plant studies. Fig. 2 shows such a unit along with the piping (flow) connections. Compared to a single module, the distillate feeds into the two modules on the tube side can be countercurrent or concurrent in a singlepair unit. In our studies, countercurrent mode was mostly used in order to potentially achieve a higher water vapor flux. The reason for such a choice will be discussed later. A few singlepair units of crossflow DCMD modules can be connected to each other to form a multi-stage cascade. Schematic of such a cascade with individual stream flow directions is shown in Fig. 4. Each single-pair unit may be considered as a stage in the cascade.

However, the calculation will start from the other end on the tube side (the distillate feed inlet) for the second module in a single-pair unit as the distillate feeds flow into the modules in a countercurrent mode.

texts.orig: The frame modules were only rectangular channels having coated hollow fibers running along the frame length with two open faces. A diverging section and a converging section were designed and fabricated to allow the liquid to flow uniformly in crossflow outside of and perpendicular to the fibers. The assembly design developed at NJIT was the same as that used earlier [2]. The diverging section and the converging section were two rectangular boxes having a curved shape. Two face plates were made from two flat translucent polypropylene plastic sheets. On each face plate, 104 smaller holes having a wide size distribution were opened (the holes at the center were smaller, while those further away were progressively larger). Such a design described above was meant to ensure uniform feed brine flow through the shell side of the fibers.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A complete membrane device consisted of two face boxes and face plates assembled with a rectangular membrane module channel and 16 bolts. Neoprene gaskets (1/8 ′′ ) were used between the face box, the face plate and the module channel on each side to seal the parts together. Two rectangular modules can be stacked one on top of the other as a single-pair unit for testing (Fig. 2). In the pilot plantstudiesthiswasthemostcommontestingconfigurations.One Neoprene gasket was placed between the two rectangular mem- brane modules when a single-pair unit was used for testing. Hot brine was allowed to enter one face box, leave the box through the face plate holes which distributed the liquid flow evenly, and then enter the flow channel. On the brine exit side, the liquid left the channel through the face plate holes and collected in the face box and then flowed beyond the box and thus the module. Between the face plate and the frame containing the fibers, there was some space due to the gasket. In addition, there was a small axial gap around 1/4 ′′ between the module frame surface and the beginning of the fibers. This allowed evening out of any irregularity in the flow out of the face plate into the fibers. The liquid flow was expected to cross the fiber layer uniformly and perpendicularly to ensure good heat and mass transfer.

texts.orig: A Cahn DCA315 (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA 02454) balanceatUTRCwasusedtocharacterizethedynamiccontactangle of water on hollow fiber membrane surfaces. The measurements facilitate understanding the effects of various parameters such as temperature, source and concentration of contaminants, thermal cycle, etc. on the hydrophobicity of the membrane surfaces. Cahn DCA 315 employing Wilhelmy technique [13] measures advancing (dry surface) contact angle as well as the receding (wet surface) contact angle by monitoring the wetting forces during advancing and receding of water interacting with samples. The samples were treated under a controlled environment and tested using the DCA balance. Before the contact angle measurement, the equipment was calibrated with distilled water.

texts.orig: For a single-pair unit (two modules-one stacked on top of the other), there are two test configurations (Fig. 15) by switching the distillate flow directions: countercurrent when the distillate flows in opposite direction; concurrent when the distillate flows in the samedirection.Thehotbrinefeedcontinuouslypenetratesthefiber layers from module one (MD-1) to module two (MD-2). The hot brine was assumed to be evenly distributed along the fiber length at a uniform temperature for the first fiber layer. In the brine flow direction, the hot brine was cooled down by the sensible heat loss due to the latent heat needed for water evaporation and sensitive heat loss from conduction. The heat was transferred to the distillate side and the distillate got heated up in the distillate flow direction. Therefore, a higher water vapor flux was achieved at the distillate inlet (compared to that at the distillate outlet) due to a larger temperature difference between the hot brine and the distillate. Correspondingly, a hot brine temperature gradient was created at the outlet in the x -direction from the first fiber layer: the shell-side brine temperature at the distillate outlet is higher than that at the distillate inlet. It means lower brine inlet temperature for the next fiber layer at the distillate inlet and higher shell-side brine inlet temperature at the distillate outlet. However, the water vapor flux at the distillate inlet could be still higher than that at the distillate outlet due to a much lower distillate temperature at the inlet; the water vapor flux difference, however, became smaller.

texts.orig: The outlet hot brine temperature profile would be dependent on the feed conditions (temperatures and flow rates) of both the hot brine and cold distillate. Consider the case where the shell-side brine temperature at the outlet of the first module was higher at the distillate outlet than that at the distillate inlet and without lateral mixing the brine continuously entered into the second module. Higher average water vapor flux may be achieved when the distillate flows into the second module (MD-2) countercurrently. Fig. 16 presents the experimental results for a single-pair unit of these two test configurations by varying the brine feed flow rate. Minor difference was seen from the water vapor flux achieved for all the feed conditions. However, it still could be seen that a higher water vapor flux was achieved for countercurrent distillate feed configuration at a lower brine flow rate. Concurrent distillate feed configuration seemed slightly better at a higher brine feed flow rate. Fig. 16 also showed that the model was able to predict such a behavior. In the pilot plant studies, the countercurrent distillate feed configuration was adopted as the most common test configuration for a single-pair unit in a DCMD cascade.

texts.orig: We have assumed in our model that there is no lateral mixing in the hot brine as it moves through the fiber bed. The photo of an experimental setup implemented at NJIT to evaluate this assumption is provided in Fig. 17a and b shows schematically the locations of eight temperature probes on a frame at the exit end of the brine flow in the module S/N 1014. The intent was to measure the hot brine temperature as it exits the fiber bed and find out whether the temperature variation predicted in the model along the distillate flow path based on the assumption of no lateral mixing was valid or not. The probes were put in two layers of four each as shown in Fig. 17b. Fig. 18 illustrates the measured temperatures in the hot brine exiting the 26th fiber layer at three hot brine velocities. This figure also has solid lines obtained from the model. It appears that in general the model predicts a somewhat higher exit brine temperature than the experimental values as the distillate flow length increases. This suggests that there is some degree of lateral mixing in the hot brine as it exits one module of 24-26 fiber layers. We have to take this mixing into account in the modeling of a cascade to improve our predictive capabilities.

texts.orig: 4. Concluding remarks
texts.text: 4. Concluding remarks

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Crossflow hollow fiber membrane modules used in our earlier membrane distillation studies [1,2,7] were successfully scaled up from 119cm 2 to 0.28 m 2 to larger modules each having a membrane surface area of 0.66 m 2 in the pilot plant. The contact angle measurement indicated that the hydrophobicity of the outside surfaces of the hollow fibers was reduced somewhat by being exposed to the salt solutions and high temperature. Further understanding such an effect would require more experimental studies. The total membrane surface area was successfully varied between 0.66m 2 and 6.6 m 2 for a variety of feed temperatures of the hot brine and the distillate.
texts.text: Crossflow hollow fiber membrane modules used in our earlier membrane distillation studies [1,2,7] were successfully scaled up from 119cm 2 to 0.28 m 2 to larger modules each having a membrane surface area of 0.66 m 2 in the pilot plant. The contact angle measurement indicated that the hydrophobicity of the outside surfaces of the hollow fibers was reduced somewhat by being exposed to the salt solutions and high temperature. Further understanding such an effect would require more experimental studies. The total membrane surface area was successfully varied between 0.66m 2 and 6.6 m 2 for a variety of feed temperatures of the hot brine and the distillate.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The pilot plant was successfully operated for 3 months. The DCMD performance of the pilot plant was studied for various test configurations employing concurrent or countercurrent distillate feeds, or multiple modules and/or brine feed splits. The pilot plant achieved a productivity of 0.62 gpm (2.35 LPM) distillate production rate. The average flux at this level of production varied between 15 to 33 kg/(m 2 h) (8.8-19.4 gfd). An additional configuration indicated a potential production capacity of 0.95 gpm (3.6 LPM). Less than 1 ppm salt concentration from the CSTR calculations indicated that highly pure distilled water was produced from a 10% NaCl solution. Therefore, DCMD can potentially be an alternative desali- nation technology to meet ultrapure water needs for industries, such as pharmaceuticals, semiconductor. Extended-term operation of the pilot plant for sea water desalination was successfully carried out. High percent recovery of water was achieved and relatively stable water vapor flux was obtained when sea water was concentrated from 2.9% salt to 19% before the scaling salts precipitated. Simple water cleaning recovered the DCMD performance of the crossflow modules. It would be reasonable to postulate that DCMD can be either an alternative desalination technology for producing drinking water or integrated with other traditional desalination technologies (e.g., reverse osmosis) for higher water recovery. Integration of DCMD with other desalination technologies will be a step toward zero liquid discharge (ZLD) technology; it will reduce the concentrate volume, lower the concentrate treatment/transportation cost and minimize its environmental effect.
texts.text: The pilot plant was successfully operated for 3 months. The DCMD performance of the pilot plant was studied for various test configurations employing concurrent or countercurrent distillate feeds, or multiple modules and/or brine feed splits. The pilot plant achieved a productivity of 0.62 gpm (2.35 LPM) distillate production rate. The average flux at this level of production varied between 15 to 33 kg/(m 2 h) (8.8-19.4 gfd). An additional configuration indicated a potential production capacity of 0.95 gpm (3.6 LPM). Less than 1 ppm salt concentration from the CSTR calculations indicated that highly pure distilled water was produced from a 10% NaCl solution. Therefore, DCMD can potentially be an alternative desali- nation technology to meet ultrapure water needs for industries, such as pharmaceuticals, semiconductor. Extended-term operation of the pilot plant for sea water desalination was successfully carried out. High percent recovery of water was achieved and relatively stable water vapor flux was obtained when sea water was concentrated from 2.9% salt to 19% before the scaling salts precipitated. Simple water cleaning recovered the DCMD performance of the crossflow modules. It would be reasonable to postulate that DCMD can be either an alternative desalination technology for producing drinking water or integrated with other traditional desalination technologies (e.g., reverse osmosis) for higher water recovery. Integration of DCMD with other desalination technologies will be a step toward zero liquid discharge (ZLD) technology; it will reduce the concentrate volume, lower the concentrate treatment/transportation cost and minimize its environmental effect.
